title: Determinants
quote: All Mathematical truths are relative and conditional. -- C.P. Steinmetz

sections.title: Introduction
concepts: A system of linear equations can be expressed in matrix form
For a system a1*x + b1*y = c1 and a2*x + b2*y = c2, the matrix form is [a1 b1; a2 b2][x; y] = [c1; c2]
The system has a unique solution if a1*b2 - a2*b1 != 0
The number a1*b2 - a2*b1 which determines uniqueness of solution is associated with the matrix A and is called the determinant of A
key_points: Determinants have wide applications in Engineering, Science, Economics, Social Science, etc.
In this chapter, determinants up to order three with real entries only are studied

Topics covered: properties of determinants, minors, cofactors, applications in finding area of triangle, adjoint and inverse of a square matrix, consistency and inconsistency of systems, solution using inverse of a matrix

sections.title: Determinant
concepts: To every square matrix A = [a_ij] of order n, we can associate a number (real or complex) called determinant of the square matrix A
Determinant is a function which associates each square matrix with a unique number
If M is the set of square matrices, K is the set of numbers and f: M -> K is defined by f(A) = k, then k = f(A) is called the determinant of A
Denoted by |A| or det A or Delta
key_points: For matrix A, |A| is read as determinant of A and not modulus of A
Only square matrices have determinants

subsections.title: Determinant of a matrix of order one
concepts: Let A = [a], then determinant of A is defined to be equal to a
formulas: |A| = a, where A = [a] is a 1x1 matrix

subsections.title: Determinant of a matrix of order two
concepts: For a 2x2 matrix, the determinant is computed as product of diagonal elements minus product of off-diagonal elements

subsections.title: Determinant of a matrix of order 3x3
concepts: A 3x3 determinant can be determined by expressing it in terms of second order determinants
This is known as expansion of a determinant along a row or column
There are six ways of expanding a determinant of order 3: along R1, R2, R3, C1, C2, C3
Expanding a determinant along any row or column gives the same value
formulas: General term sign: (-1)^(i+j) for element a_ij
If A = kB where A and B are square matrices of order n, then |A| = k^n * |B|
key_points: For easier calculations, expand along the row or column which contains maximum number of zeros
Instead of multiplying by (-1)^(i+j), multiply by +1 or -1 according as (i+j) is even or odd

examples.solution: In the third column, two entries are zero. Expanding along C3: Delta = 4|(-1) 3; 4 1| - 0|1 2; 4 1| + 0|1 2; -1 3| = 4(-1-12) - 0 + 0 = 4(-13) = -52
Expanding along R1: Delta = 0 - sin(alpha)(-sin(alpha)*sin(beta)*cos(alpha) - 0) - cos(alpha)(-sin(alpha)*(-sin(beta)) - 0) = sin(alpha)*sin(beta)*cos(alpha) - cos(alpha)*sin(alpha)*sin(beta) = 0

examples.problem: Find values of x for which |3  x; x  1| = |3  2; 4  1|

sections.title: Area of a Triangle
concepts: The area of a triangle with vertices (x1, y1), (x2, y2), (x3, y3) can be expressed using determinants
Since area is positive, we always take the absolute value of the determinant
If area is given, use both positive and negative values of the determinant for calculation
The area of the triangle formed by three collinear points is zero
formulas: \Delta = \frac{1}{2} \begin{vmatrix} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{vmatrix}
\begin{vmatrix} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = 0 \; (\text{collinear})
key_points: Always take absolute value since area is a positive quantity
If area is given, use both positive and negative values of the determinant
Collinearity condition: area = 0 implies three points are collinear

examples.problem: Find the area of the triangle whose vertices are (3, 8), (-4, 2) and (5, 1)
Find the equation of the line joining A(1, 3) and B(0, 0) using determinants and find k if D(k, 0) is a point such that area of triangle ABD is 3 sq units
examples.solution: Let P(x, y) be any point on AB. Area of triangle ABP is zero: (1/2)|0  0  1; 1  3  1; x  y  1| = 0. This gives (1/2)(y - 3x) = 0, so y = 3x. For area of ABD = 3: (1/2)|1  3  1; 0  0  1; k  0  1| = +/- 3. This gives -3k/2 = +/- 3, so k = -/+ 2.

sections.title: Minors and Cofactors
concepts: Minors and cofactors are used to write the expansion of a determinant in compact form
The determinant can be expressed as sum of products of elements of any row (or column) with their corresponding cofactors
If elements of a row (or column) are multiplied with cofactors of any other row (or column), the sum is zero

definitions.definition: Minor of an element a_ij of a determinant is the determinant obtained by deleting its ith row and jth column in which element a_ij lies. Minor of element a_ij is denoted by M_ij.
Cofactor of an element a_ij, denoted by A_ij, is defined by A_ij = (-1)^(i+j) * M_ij, where M_ij is the minor of a_ij.

formulas: Minor of an element of a determinant of order n (n >= 2) is a determinant of order n-1
key_points: Delta = sum of product of elements of any row (or column) with their corresponding cofactors
If elements of a row (or column) are multiplied with cofactors of any other row (or column), the sum is zero
For example, a11*A21 + a12*A22 + a13*A23 = 0 (R1 elements with R2 cofactors gives zero since it creates a determinant with two identical rows)

examples.problem: Find the minor of element 6 in the determinant Delta = |1  2  3; 4  5  6; 7  8  9|
examples.solution: Since 6 lies in R2, C3, its minor M_23 = |1  2; 7  8| = 8 - 14 = -6

examples.problem: Find minors and cofactors of all elements of the determinant |1  -2; 4  3|
Find minors and cofactors of elements a_11 and a_21 in |a11  a12  a13; a21  a22  a23; a31  a32  a33|
Find minors and cofactors of elements of |2  -3  5; 6  0  4; 1  5  -7| and verify a11*A31 + a12*A32 + a13*A33 = 0

sections.title: Adjoint and Inverse of a Matrix
concepts: The adjoint of a square matrix is the transpose of the matrix of cofactors
A matrix is singular if its determinant is zero
A matrix is non-singular if its determinant is non-zero
A square matrix is invertible if and only if it is non-singular
The inverse of a matrix can be found using the adjoint

definitions.definition: The adjoint of a square matrix A = [a_ij]_(n x n) is defined as the transpose of the matrix [A_ij]_(n x n), where A_ij is the cofactor of element a_ij. It is denoted by adj A.
A square matrix A is said to be singular if |A| = 0.
A square matrix A is said to be non-singular if |A| != 0.

formulas: For 2x2 matrix A = [a11 a12; a21 a22], adj A = [a22 -a12; -a21 a11] (interchange diagonal elements, change sign of off-diagonal elements)
If AB = BA = I, then B is called the inverse of A, i.e., B = A^(-1)

theorems.statement: If A be any given square matrix of order n, then A(adj A) = (adj A)A = |A|*I, where I is the identity matrix of order n.
If A and B are nonsingular matrices of the same order, then AB and BA are also nonsingular matrices of the same order.
The determinant of the product of matrices is equal to product of their respective determinants, that is, |AB| = |A| * |B|, where A and B are square matrices of the same order.
A square matrix A is invertible if and only if A is nonsingular matrix.

key_points: A(adj A) = (adj A)A = |A|I
A square matrix has inverse if and only if it is non-singular (|A| != 0)
For 2x2 matrix: interchange diagonal elements and change signs of off-diagonal elements to get adjoint

examples.problem: If A = [1  3  3; 1  4  3; 1  3  4], verify that A(adj A) = |A|I. Also find A^(-1).
Show that matrix A = [2  3; 1  2] satisfies A^2 - 4A + I = O. Using this equation, find A^(-1).

sections.title: Applications of Determinants and Matrices
concepts: Determinants and matrices are used for solving systems of linear equations in two or three variables
Used for checking consistency of system of linear equations
System of equations can be written in matrix form AX = B

definitions.definition: A system of equations is said to be consistent if its solution (one or more) exists.
A system of equations is said to be inconsistent if its solution does not exist.

formulas: For system: a1*x + b1*y + c1*z = d1, a2*x + b2*y + c2*z = d2, a3*x + b3*y + c3*z = d3: Matrix form AX = B where A = [a1 b1 c1; a2 b2 c2; a3 b3 c3], X = [x; y; z], B = [d1; d2; d3]
If |A| = 0 and (adj A)B != O, system is inconsistent (no solution)
If |A| = 0 and (adj A)B = O, system may be consistent (infinitely many solutions) or inconsistent
key_points: Case I: |A| != 0 => A is nonsingular => inverse exists => unique solution X = A^(-1)*B
Case II: |A| = 0 => A is singular => compute (adj A)B
If (adj A)B != O (zero matrix), then no solution exists and system is inconsistent
If (adj A)B = O, then system may be either consistent (infinitely many solutions) or inconsistent

subsections.title: Solution of system of linear equations using inverse of a matrix

examples.problem: Solve by matrix method: 3x - 2y + 3z = 8, 2x + y - z = 1, 4x - 3y + 2z = 4

The sum of three numbers is 6. If we multiply third number by 3 and add second number to it, we get 11. By adding first and third numbers, we get double of the second number. Find the numbers using matrix method.

examples.solution: The product of the two matrices = I (identity matrix). Hence [1  -1  2; 0  2  -3; 3  -2  4]^(-1) = [-2  0  1; 9  2  -3; 6  1  -2]. The system AX = B gives X = A^(-1)*B = [-2  0  1; 9  2  -3; 6  1  -2][1; 1; 2] = [0; 5; 3]. Hence x = 0, y = 5, z = 3.

summary: Determinant of a matrix A = [a_11] of order 1: |a_11| = a_11
Determinant of a 2x2 matrix A = [a11 a12; a21 a22]: |A| = a11*a22 - a12*a21
Determinant of a 3x3 matrix (expanding along R1): |A| = a1|b2 c2; b3 c3| - b1|a2 c2; a3 c3| + c1|a2 b2; a3 b3|
For any square matrix A, the |A| satisfies certain properties
Area of triangle with vertices (x1,y1), (x2,y2), (x3,y3): Delta = (1/2)|x1 y1 1; x2 y2 1; x3 y3 1|
Minor of element a_ij is the determinant obtained by deleting ith row and jth column, denoted M_ij
Value of determinant = sum of product of elements of a row (or column) with corresponding cofactors. E.g., |A| = a11*A11 + a12*A12 + a13*A13
If elements of one row (or column) are multiplied with cofactors of any other row (or column), their sum is zero. E.g., a11*A21 + a12*A22 + a13*A23 = 0
For 3x3 matrix A, adj A = [A11 A21 A31; A12 A22 A32; A13 A23 A33] (transpose of cofactor matrix), where A_ij is cofactor of a_ij
A(adj A) = (adj A)A = |A|*I, where A is a square matrix of order n
A square matrix A is singular or non-singular according as |A| = 0 or |A| != 0
If AB = BA = I (where B is a square matrix), then B is called inverse of A. Also A^(-1) = B or B^(-1) = A, and (A^(-1))^(-1) = A
A square matrix A has inverse if and only if A is non-singular
For system a1*x + b1*y + c1*z = d1, a2*x + b2*y + c2*z = d2, a3*x + b3*y + c3*z = d3: these can be written as AX = B
Unique solution of AX = B is given by X = A^(-1)*B, where |A| != 0
A system of equations is consistent or inconsistent according as its solution exists or not

For square matrix A in matrix equation AX = B: (i) |A| != 0 => unique solution exists, (ii) |A| = 0 and (adj A)B != O => no solution exists, (iii) |A| = 0 and (adj A)B = O => system may or may not be consistent

questions.question: Evaluate the determinant |2  4; -5  -1|.

(i) Evaluate |cos(theta)  -sin(theta); sin(theta)  cos(theta)|.
(ii) Evaluate |x^2-x+1  x-1; x+1  x+1|.
If A = [1  2; 4  2], then show that |2A| = 4|A|.
If A = [1  0  1; 0  1  2; 0  0  4], then show that |3A| = 27|A|.

If |x  2; 18  x| = |6  2; 18  6|, then x is equal to:
(A) 6
(B) +/- 6
(C) -6
(D) 0

Find area of the triangle with vertices at the point given in each of the following:
(i) (1, 0), (6, 0), (4, 3)
(ii) (2, 7), (1, 1), (10, 8)
(iii) (-2, -3), (3, 2), (-1, -8)
questions.answer: (i) 15/2 sq units, (ii) 47/2 sq units, (iii) 15 sq units

questions.question: Show that points A(a, b+c), B(b, c+a), C(c, a+b) are collinear.
questions.answer: Hint: Apply R2 -> R2 - R1, R3 -> R3 - R1, then show determinant = 0

questions.question:
Find values of k if area of triangle is 4 sq. units and vertices are:
(i) (k, 0), (4, 0), (0, 2)
(ii) (-2, 0), (0, 4), (0, k)
questions.answer: (i) k = 0 or k = 8, (ii) k = -4 or k = 12

questions.question:
(i) Find equation of line joining (1, 2) and (3, 6) using determinants.
(ii) Find equation of line joining (3, 1) and (9, 3) using determinants.

If area of triangle is 35 sq units with vertices (2, -6), (5, 4) and (k, 4). Then k is:
(A) 12
(B) -2
(C) -12, -2
(D) 12, -2

Write Minors and Cofactors of the elements of following determinants:
(i) |2  -4; 0  3|
(ii) |a  c; b  d|

Write Minors and Cofactors of the elements of following determinants:
(i) |1  0  0; 0  1  0; 0  0  1|
(ii) |1  0  4; 3  5  -1; 0  1  2|
Using Cofactors of elements of second row, evaluate Delta = |5  3  8; 2  0  1; 1  2  3|.
Using Cofactors of elements of third column, evaluate Delta = |1  x  yz; 1  y  zx; 1  z  xy|.

If Delta = |a11  a12  a13; a21  a22  a23; a31  a32  a33| and A_ij is Cofactors of a_ij, then value of Delta is given by:
(A) a11*A31 + a12*A32 + a13*A33
(B) a11*A11 + a12*A21 + a13*A31
(C) a21*A11 + a22*A12 + a23*A13
(D) a11*A11 + a21*A21 + a31*A31
Find adjoint of the matrix [1  2; 3  4].
Find adjoint of the matrix [1  -1  2; 2  3  5; -2  0  1].
Verify A(adj A) = (adj A)A = |A|I for the matrix [2  3; -4  -6].
Verify A(adj A) = (adj A)A = |A|I for the matrix [1  -1  2; 3  0  -2; 1  0  3].
questions.answer: Hint: |A| = 1(0+0) + 1(9+2) + 2(0-0) = 11. Compute cofactor matrix, transpose to get adj A, then verify both products equal 11*I

questions.question: Find the inverse of the matrix (if it exists): [2  -2; 4  3].
Find the inverse of the matrix (if it exists): [-1  5; -3  2].
Find the inverse of the matrix (if it exists): [1  2  3; 0  2  4; 0  0  5].
Find the inverse of the matrix (if it exists): [1  0  0; 3  3  0; 5  2  -1].
Find the inverse of the matrix (if it exists): [2  1  3; 4  -1  0; -7  2  1].
Find the inverse of the matrix (if it exists): [1  -1  2; 0  2  -3; 3  -2  4].
Find the inverse of the matrix (if it exists): [1  0  0; 0  cos(alpha)  sin(alpha); 0  sin(alpha)  -cos(alpha)].
questions.answer: [1  0  0; 0  cos(alpha)  sin(alpha); 0  sin(alpha)  -cos(alpha)] (the matrix is its own inverse since |A| = -1 and A^2 = I)

questions.question: Let A = [3  7; 2  5] and B = [6  8; 7  9]. Verify that (AB)^(-1) = B^(-1)*A^(-1).
questions.answer: Hint: Find AB, then compute (AB)^(-1) directly and compare with B^(-1)*A^(-1). |A| = 1, |B| = -2

questions.question: If A = [3  1; -1  2], show that A^2 - 5A + 7I = O. Hence find A^(-1).
For the matrix A = [3  2; 1  1], find the numbers a and b such that A^2 + aA + bI = O.
For the matrix A = [1  1  1; 1  2  -3; 2  -1  3], show that A^3 - 6A^2 + 5A + 11I = O. Hence, find A^(-1).
If A = [2  -1  1; -1  2  -1; 1  -1  2], verify that A^3 - 6A^2 + 9A - 4I = O and hence find A^(-1).

Let A be a nonsingular square matrix of order 3 x 3. Then |adj A| is equal to:
(A) |A|
(B) |A|^2
(C) |A|^3
(D) 3|A|

If A is an invertible matrix of order 2, then det(A^(-1)) is equal to:
(A) det(A)
(B) 1/det(A)
(C) 1
(D) 0

Examine the consistency of the system of equations:
x + 2y = 2
2x + 3y = 3

Examine the consistency of the system of equations:
2x - y = 5
x + y = 4

Examine the consistency of the system of equations:
x + 3y = 5
2x + 6y = 8
questions.answer: Inconsistent. |A| = 0 and (adj A)B is nonzero, so no solution exists

questions.question:
Examine the consistency of the system of equations:
x + y + z = 1
2x + 3y + 2z = 2
ax + ay + 2az = 4
questions.answer: Consistent. |A| = a (nonzero for a != 0), unique solution: x = 0, y = 0, z = 1. Note: the third equation is a(x + y + 2z) = 4, so a != 0

questions.question:
Examine the consistency of the system of equations:
3x - y - 2z = 2
2y - z = -1
3x - 5y = 3

Examine the consistency of the system of equations:
5x - y + 4z = 5
2x + 3y + 5z = 2
5x - 2y + 6z = -1
questions.answer: Consistent. |A| = 0, but (adj A)B = O, so system has infinitely many solutions

questions.question:
Solve system of linear equations, using matrix method:
5x + 2y = 4
7x + 3y = 5

Solve system of linear equations, using matrix method:
2x - y = -2
3x + 4y = 3

Solve system of linear equations, using matrix method:
4x - 3y = 3
3x - 5y = 7

Solve system of linear equations, using matrix method:
5x + 2y = 3
3x + 2y = 5

Solve system of linear equations, using matrix method:
2x + y + z = 1
x - 2y - z = 3/2
3y - 5z = 9

Solve system of linear equations, using matrix method:
x - y + z = 4
2x + y - 3z = 0
x + y + z = 2

Solve system of linear equations, using matrix method:
2x + 3y + 3z = 5
x - 2y + z = -4
3x - y - 2z = 3

Solve system of linear equations, using matrix method:
x - y + 2z = 7
3x + 4y - 5z = -5
2x - y + 3z = 12

If A = [2  -3  5; 3  2  -4; 1  1  -2], find A^(-1). Using A^(-1) solve the system of equations:
2x - 3y + 5z = 11
3x + 2y - 4z = -5
x + y - 2z = -3

The cost of 4 kg onion, 3 kg wheat and 2 kg rice is Rs 60. The cost of 2 kg onion, 4 kg wheat and 6 kg rice is Rs 90. The cost of 6 kg onion, 2 kg wheat and 3 kg rice is Rs 70. Find cost of each item per kg by matrix method.
questions.answer: Onion = Rs 5/kg, Wheat = Rs 8/kg, Rice = Rs 8/kg

questions.question: Prove that the determinant |x  sin(theta)  cos(theta); -sin(theta)  -x  1; cos(theta)  1  x| is independent of theta.
questions.answer: Hint: Expand along R1 to get -x^3 - x + x(sin^2(theta) + cos^2(theta)) = -x^3 - x + x = -x(x^2), i.e., determinant = -(x^3 + x), which has no theta

questions.question: Evaluate |cos(alpha)cos(beta)  cos(alpha)sin(beta)  -sin(alpha); -sin(beta)  cos(beta)  0; sin(alpha)cos(beta)  sin(alpha)sin(beta)  cos(alpha)|.

Let A = [1  2  1; 2  3  1; 1  1  5]. Verify that:
(i) [adj A]^(-1) = adj(A^(-1))
(ii) (A^(-1))^(-1) = A
questions.answer: Hint: |A| = -6. Find adj A, then compute both sides and verify equality

questions.question: Evaluate |x  y  x+y; y  x+y  x; x+y  x  y|.
Evaluate |1  x  y; 1  x+y  y; 1  x  x+y|.

Solve the system of equations:
2/x + 3/y + 10/z = 4
4/x - 6/y + 5/z = 1
6/x + 9/y - 20/z = 2

If x, y, z are nonzero real numbers, then the inverse of matrix A = [x  0  0; 0  y  0; 0  0  z] is:
(A) [x^(-1)  0  0; 0  y^(-1)  0; 0  0  z^(-1)]
(B) xyz*[x^(-1)  0  0; 0  y^(-1)  0; 0  0  z^(-1)]
(C) (1/xyz)*[x  0  0; 0  y  0; 0  0  z]
(D) (1/xyz)*[1  0  0; 0  1  0; 0  0  1]

Let A = [1  sin(theta)  1; -sin(theta)  1  sin(theta); -1  -sin(theta)  1], where 0 <= theta <= 2*pi. Then:
(A) Det(A) = 0
(B) Det(A) is in (2, infinity)
(C) Det(A) is in (2, 4)
(D) Det(A) is in [2, 4]
